chapters.label: Modals: could, might
chapters.subtitle: I could swim · It might rain · Possibility and past ability

slides.intro: 'Could' is the past form of 'can' for ability, OR a present possibility.
'Might' = maybe. Same form for all subjects.
slides.watch_out: After 'might' and 'could', use the BASE verb. NOT 'might to go' or 'could goes'.

rows: Past ability OR present possibility (similar to might)
Only present/future possibility, not past ability

questions.q: She _____ come tonight — she's not sure.
He _____ read when he was 4 — he was advanced.
When I was a child, I _____ ride a bike easily.
It _____ be true — I'm not sure.
_____ you pass the salt, please? (polite request)
She _____ come — she has another plan.

game.meaning: past form of can — for past ability

chapters.label: Modals: must, should
chapters.subtitle: You must stop · You should rest · Obligation and advice

slides.intro: 'Must' = something is necessary or required. Strong obligation, often a rule.
rows: 'Have to' is similar but more for external rules

slides.intro: 'Should' = it's a good idea. Weaker than 'must'.

slides.watch_out: After 'must' and 'should' use the BASE verb. NOT 'must to' or 'should to'.

questions.q: You _____ stop at the red light. (rule)
We _____ leave early to avoid traffic.
You _____ go to the doctor — you look very ill.
He _____ try harder — he's failing all his subjects.
You _____ wear a helmet — it's the law.
You _____ eat junk food — it's bad for your health.

slides.intro: For one or two-syllable adjectives, add -er + than to compare.
For 2+ syllable adjectives, use more + adjective + than.
rows: more difficult, more expensive, more comfortable

slides.intro: Some adjectives are irregular — must memorise.
slides.watch_out: NEVER use 'more' with -er adjectives. Don't say 'more bigger' or 'more better'. Just 'bigger' or 'better'.

questions.q: This book is _____ than that one. (interesting)
This car is _____ than that one. (expensive)

game.meaning: adjectives ending in y change to ier
short word, single vowel + consonant → double

chapters.label: Countable & Uncountable
chapters.subtitle: an apple · some water · How much / How many?

slides.intro: Countable = you can count them. They have singular and plural forms.
rows: an apple, a book, a car

slides.intro: Uncountable = you cannot count them directly. No plural form, no a/an.

rows: A lot of apples / a lot of water
slides.watch_out: NEVER use 'a/an' with uncountable nouns. Don't say 'a water' or 'an information'. Use 'some water' or 'a glass of water'.

questions.q: How _____ water is in the bottle?
There are _____ books on the table.
I bought _____ apple at the market.

ex2.title: Complete with much, many, some, a or an

questions.q: There are _____ books on the shelf.
She bought _____ orange at the market.

game.meaning: cannot count - no plural, no a/an

chapters.label: Quantifiers
chapters.subtitle: a lot of · much · many · few · little

slides.h2: a lot of / lots of — both
slides.intro: 'A lot of' or 'lots of' work with both countable and uncountable nouns.
'Many' = countable. 'Much' = uncountable. Mostly used in negatives and questions.
rows: Use 'a lot of' instead of 'much/many' in positive sentences

slides.h2: few / a few · little / a little
slides.intro: Small quantity. 'a few/little' = a small positive amount. 'few/little' = almost none (negative).
rows: I have a few friends here. (some, positive)
slides.watch_out: few/a few = countable plural. little/a little = uncountable. Don't mix them up.

rows: a lot of, lots of, some

questions.q: There isn't _____ milk in the fridge.
She has _____ friends — she's very popular.
I have _____ time, sorry — I have to go.
There are _____ people in the park today. (many)
He has _____ patience — he's very calm. (positive)
I have _____ books I want to read. (some)
Just _____ milk in my coffee, please. (small amount)

game.meaning: large quantity, both countable and uncountable
game.example: A lot of people · a lot of water

chapters.label: Too & Enough
chapters.subtitle: too cold · not warm enough · enough time

slides.intro: 'Too' = more than is good. Always negative meaning.
'Enough' = the necessary amount. Position is different from too.

slides.watch_out: With adjectives, 'too' goes BEFORE the adjective and 'enough' goes AFTER. Don't say 'enough warm' — it's 'warm enough'.

ex1.title: Choose too / too much / too many / enough

questions.q: It's _____ cold to go to the beach.
I have _____ time to finish this. (positive)
There are _____ cars in the city.
This soup is _____ hot — I can't eat it.
He's _____ young to drive — he's only 12.
Do we have _____ chairs for everyone?
This bag is _____ heavy. I can't carry it. (more than I can)
She isn't tall _____ to reach the shelf.
I have _____ many emails to read today!

chapters.label: Relative Clauses
chapters.subtitle: the man who · the book that · the place where

slides.intro: Use who to give more information about a person.
rows: The man who lives next door is a doctor.
Who is he? (question) — The man who is here (relative)
my friend who studies at uni · the woman who called

slides.intro: Use that or which for things, animals or ideas. 'That' is more common in speaking.
rows: The book that I bought is interesting.
The dog that bit me ran away.
'that' and 'which' are usually interchangeable in spoken English

slides.intro: Use where to talk about a place.
rows: This is the café where we met.
The hotel where I stayed · the city where she grew up
they connect two ideas: 'The man. He is a doctor.' = 'The man who is a doctor.'

questions.q: The woman _____ lives next door is a teacher.
This is the book _____ I told you about.
That's the café _____ we had lunch.
He's the boy _____ won the prize.
The phone _____ I bought yesterday is broken.
This is the hospital _____ I was born.
The actor _____ played the lead is famous.
I love the city _____ my grandmother lives.
The girl _____ called you is my cousin.
This is the restaurant _____ we celebrated my birthday.
The car _____ I want is too expensive.
That's the teacher _____ teaches us maths.

game.completion: The book ______ I read = the book that I read.

game.meaning: join two sentences with a relative pronoun
game.example: 'The man. He is a doctor.' → 'The man who is a doctor.'
The girl who sings (NOT the girl that sings).

slides.intro: English follows a strict pattern: Subject + Verb + Object.
rows: NOT 'Eat I pizza' or 'Pizza I eat'

slides.intro: When you have BOTH place and time in a sentence, place comes first.
rows: I went to the cinema (place) yesterday (time).
NOT 'I went yesterday to the cinema'
She works in London (P) every day (T).

slides.intro: Quick reminder: always, usually, often, sometimes, never go BEFORE the main verb but AFTER 'be'.

slides.watch_out: In Spanish you can say 'come pizza yo' but in English the order MUST be I eat pizza. Subject always first.

options: She went yesterday to the park.
She went to the park yesterday.
Yesterday she went the park to.
questions.answer: She went to the park yesterday.

options: She studies English at school every day.
She at school studies English every day.
Every day she at school studies English.
questions.answer: She studies English at school every day.

options: Usually I am eat breakfast at 8am.

questions.q: to school / walks / she / every day → _____
questions.answer: She walks to school every day
accept: she walks to school every day

questions.q: in the park / play / they / on Sundays → _____
questions.answer: They play in the park on Sundays
accept: they play in the park on Sundays

game.meaning: if you have both, place comes first
always/often/never go before the main verb
with the verb 'be', adverb comes AFTER
English always starts with subject (no Spanish-style omission)

game.example: My brother works here (NOT my brother he works).
game.completion: My brother ______ works here. (just verb, no extra subject)

chapters.label: Object Pronouns
chapters.subtitle: me · you · him · her · it · us · them

slides.intro: Object pronouns receive the action. They go AFTER the verb.
Subject = does the action (before verb). Object = receives the action (after verb).
Always use object pronouns after prepositions like with, for, to, from.
slides.watch_out: NEVER use subject pronouns after prepositions. Don't say 'with I' or 'for he'. It's 'with me' and 'for him'.

rows: I, you, he, she, it, we, they
me, you, him, her, it, us, them
I see her. He calls them.

ex2.title: Replace the noun with a pronoun

questions.q: They called my brother and me. → They called _____.
I bought the book. → I bought _____.

chapters.label: Short Answers
chapters.subtitle: Yes, I am · No, she doesn't · Yes, they were

slides.intro: In English, we don't just say 'yes' or 'no'. We add the auxiliary verb. This sounds polite and natural.
For questions with 'be', repeat the form of 'be'.
rows: Yes, I am. / No, I'm not.
Yes, he is. / No, he isn't.
Yes, they were. / No, they weren't.

slides.intro: For other verbs in the present, use do/does. In the past, use did.
rows: Yes, I do. / No, I don't.
Yes, she does. / No, she doesn't.
Yes, I did. / No, I didn't.
Yes, I have. / No, I haven't.
slides.watch_out: NEVER repeat the main verb. Don't say 'Yes, I drink' — say 'Yes, I do'. Don't say 'No, she doesn't drink' — short version is 'No, she doesn't'.

rows: use the same be form: am/is/are/was/were
Yes, + subject + auxiliary / No, + subject + auxiliary + n't

questions.q: Does he live here? — Yes, he _____.
Have you seen it? — No, I _____.

game.meaning: never repeat the main verb in short answers
game.example: Yes, I do. (NOT yes, I drink coffee)
game.completion: Do you drink coffee? Yes, I ______. (just auxiliary)

slides.intro: These join two ideas of equal importance.
Because introduces the reason for something.
rows: reason can come first OR second
She's happy because she passed. — Because she passed, she's happy.

slides.intro: So introduces the result or consequence.
rows: It was raining, so we stayed home.
I was hungry, so I ate. (eating = result of hunger)
slides.watch_out: because vs so are opposites. 'I was tired because I worked' (reason) vs 'I worked, so I was tired' (result).

questions.q: It was raining _____ we stayed home. (result)
She's happy _____ she passed her exam.
He was sleepy _____ he went to bed.
I want to study _____ I want to relax. (contrast)
I'm cold _____ I'll wear a jacket.
He didn't come _____ he was sick.

game.example: Tired because I worked. Worked, so I'm tired.
game.completion: I worked late, ______ I'm tired now. (result)

chapters.label: Adverbs of Manner
chapters.subtitle: quickly · carefully · well · how something is done

slides.intro: Adverbs of manner describe HOW we do something. They modify a verb.
Most adverbs are formed by adding -ly to an adjective.
Some adverbs are completely different from their adjectives.
rows: 'good' is an adjective. 'well' is the adverb.
slides.watch_out: 'Good' is an adjective. 'Well' is the adverb. 'She sings well' (NOT she sings good). Also, 'hardly' means 'almost not' — completely different from 'hard'!

questions.q: The kids played _____ in the garden.

game.meaning: hard = with effort, hardly = almost not
game.example: He works hard. He hardly works = almost not.

game.meaning: adverb usually goes after the verb

chapters.label: Prepositions of Movement
chapters.subtitle: to · into · out of · across · through

slides.intro: Use to for movement towards a destination.
Movement INSIDE or OUTSIDE.

rows: Walk across the street. (from one side to the other)

slides.watch_out: home, here, there don't take 'to'. Don't say 'go to home'. Just 'go home'.

questions.q: I go _____ home now. (NO preposition needed)
We walked _____ the beach. (followed the length)
The bird flew _____ the open window. (passed inside)

game.meaning: from one side to the other
go home, not go to home
